title: 1. The World Will Be Full of Fear | The Hollywood Hack
format: Chapter 1 of Brian Raftery's 'The Hollywood Hack,' handed the Big Picture feed for a week. It opens on Obama's December 2014 press conference and rewinds to the 2014 Sony breach: the studio lot, Amy Pascal's run from 'Groundhog Day' to 'Spider-Man,' Michael Linton's business-side rule, and a slate falling behind Marvel. Then the morning of November 24, when a red skeleton and a Guardians of Peace threat locked 7,000 computers, and the first leaks over Thanksgiving spilled 'Fury' and an unreleased 'Annie' online. Ben Fritz, John Carlin, and former Sony staff Marisa McGrath-Liston, Michael Fisk, and Michelle Vio walk through it. The whole thing traces back to 'The Interview,' the Rogen-Franco Kim Jong-un comedy North Korea wanted stopped.

segments.heading: The comedy at the center of it
segments.note: the film North Korea wanted stopped

films.title: The Interview
films.note: The Seth Rogen and James Franco comedy about two TV journalists recruited to assassinate Kim Jong-un. Set to open Christmas Day 2014, it drew a threat invoking 9/11 ('the world will be full of fear'), several chains pulled it, and Sony shelved the theatrical release. Co-writer and executive producer Dan Sterling calls the whole episode 'very surreal.' John Carlin later briefed Obama on it in the Situation Room, starting with a plot synopsis.

segments.heading: The Sony lot
segments.note: a century of movies, recognizable from a single line

films.title: The Wizard of Oz
films.note: Shot on a Culver City sound stage back when MGM owned the lot; Sony's 100-foot steel rainbow sculpture is a nod to it.

films.title: North by Northwest
films.note: Cited by Raftery among the films shot steps from where he was standing.

films.title: Singin' in the Rain
films.note: Another of the lot's landmarks.

films.title: Starship Troopers
films.note: Rounding out Raftery's 'how is that not exciting' tour of the lot.

films.title: Ghostbusters
films.note: A former staffer remembers looking out her window at the Ecto-1 car parked next to the 'Breaking Bad' RV.

segments.heading: Amy Pascal's Sony
segments.note: the talent whisperer's run, hits and misses

films.title: Groundhog Day
films.note: Among the studio hits Pascal helped develop after joining in the late 1980s, when it was still Columbia.

films.title: Sense and Sensibility
films.note: Cited alongside her other early developments.

films.title: A League of Their Own
films.note: Another of the films that built her reputation.

films.title: The Da Vinci Code
films.note: A splashy literary adaptation from her run atop the movie division.

films.title: Captain Phillips
films.note: The kind of real-life drama Pascal liked to make.

films.title: Hitch
films.note: One of her crowd-pleasing comedies.

films.title: Pineapple Express
films.note: Another comedy from the same stretch.

films.title: Big Daddy
films.note: Pascal chased Adam Sandler to Atlantic City to sign him, then watched it become one of 1999's top grossers, kicking off years of Sandler money for Sony.

films.title: Forrest Gump
films.note: One of the films Pascal says she turned down, unusually candid about it: 'I've made some of the worst movies ever.'

films.title: Speed
films.note: The other pass she names in the same breath.

films.title: The Tourist
films.note: Cited as one of the genuine misfires she made for Sony.

films.title: Stealth
films.note: Another of her flops named in the episode.

films.title: Jack and Jill
films.note: The third dud in the same list; even so, some of Pascal's bad movies still made money.

segments.heading: The slate that fell behind
segments.note: star-driven Sony vs. the Marvel era

films.title: Spider-Man
films.note: Pascal's long-delayed dream project and Sony's billion-dollar franchise starter, the upside-down kiss and 'the whole Willem Dafoe-ness of it.'

films.title: Hancock
films.note: A Will Smith crowd-pleaser from Sony's star-first formula.

films.title: Click
films.note: Another A-list vehicle in the mix.

films.title: Moneyball
films.note: The grown-up dramas Sony balanced against its hits.

films.title: Zero Dark Thirty
films.note: Cited alongside 'Moneyball' as Sony's prestige side.

films.title: After Earth
films.note: A $130 million Will and Jaden Smith sci-fi drama meant to launch Sony's own cinematic universe; instead it flopped in a rough summer 2013.

films.title: Elysium
films.note: One of the summer 2013 underperformers.

films.title: White House Down
films.note: Another 2013 miss, though Raftery grants it was 'actually pretty fun.'

films.title: The Amazing Spider-Man 2
films.note: Opened at number one in May 2014 but 'ridiculously expensive, not to mention joyless' as Sony's franchise sputtered.

films.title: Sex Tape
films.note: A Cameron Diaz comedy counted among the 2014 duds.

films.title: Pompeii
films.note: The historical drama in the same run of misfires.

films.title: The Equalizer
films.note: Denzel Washington's September 2014 opener that finally gave Sony a worldwide hit and a new franchise, prompting the all-caps internal email 'I feel like we can fucking conquer the world.'

segments.heading: How the movies imagined hackers
segments.note: the cliches the real breach outran

films.title: The Net
films.note: The Sandra Bullock thriller, cited as the era's nerdy-outsider hacker cliche, and, fittingly, a Sony release.

films.title: The Social Network
films.note: 'Let the hacking begin.' Used as the 2000s thrill-seeking-brat version of hacking, also from Sony.

films.title: 22 Jump Street
films.note: A leaked email captured Channing Tatum's all-caps reaction to its big opening weekend beating a rival: 'F you, Ted! Second of all time, biatch!' (Voiced on the show by a Ringer staffer, as all the emails are.)

films.title: Ted
films.note: The 'talking teddy bear movie' Tatum was gloating past in that same email.

films.title: Fury
films.note: Brad Pitt's WWII drama, still in theaters when it leaked; downloaded more than a million times over the following days.

films.title: Annie
films.note: Sony's reported $65 million holiday musical remake, weeks from release, leaked and free to download; publicity exec Marisa McGrath-Liston's whole press tour vanished overnight.

Podcasts: Do We Get to Win This Time?
